The word resurrection usually refers to our existence beyond death. There is no trace of this teaching in the philosophies of any thinkers. The doctrine of Christ's death and resurrection is the foundation of Christianity. If it were removed, all our hopes for eternity would vanish at once. It is by holding this truth firmly that Christians stand during times of trial and are kept faithful to God. We believe in vain unless we remain faithful to the gospel. This truth is confirmed by Old Testament prophecies; and many people saw Christ after he rose from the dead. This apostle was highly favored, but always spoke humbly about himself. When sinners are, by God’s grace, transformed into saints, God causes them to remember their former sins so that they will be humble, diligent, and faithful. He gives all credit for his good qualities to God's grace. True believers, even though they understand what the Lord has done for them, in them, and through them, feel worthless when they consider their actions and obligations. All true Christians believe that Jesus Christ, especially him crucified, and then risen from the dead, is the most important part of Christianity. All the apostles agreed on this testimony; by this faith they lived, and in this faith they died.

Having shown that Christ was raised from the dead, the apostle answers those who said there would be no resurrection. There would have been no justification or salvation if Christ had not risen. And must not our faith in Christ be pointless if he remains among the dead? The proof of the resurrection of the body is the resurrection of our Lord. Even those who died believing, would have perished in their sins if Christ had not risen. All who believe in Christ have hope in him as a Redeemer; they have hope for redemption and salvation through him; but if there were no resurrection or future reward, their hope in him would only be for this life. They would be in a worse condition than the rest of humanity, especially at the time when the apostles wrote; because then Christians were hated and persecuted by everyone. But that is not the case; they, more than anyone else, experience true comfort amidst all their difficulties and trials, even during times of severe persecution.

All who are united to Christ through faith will be assured of their own resurrection because of his resurrection. Just as all people became mortal through the sin of the first Adam, because everyone inherited his sinful nature, so all who share in the Spirit and spiritual nature will revive and live forever through the resurrection of Christ. There will be an order to the resurrection. Christ himself was the first; at his coming, his redeemed people will be raised before others; last of all, the wicked will rise. Then this present world will end. If we want to triumph during that solemn and important time, we must now submit to his rule, accept his salvation, and live for his glory. Then we will rejoice in the completion of his work, so that God may receive all the glory for our salvation, so that we can forever serve him and enjoy his favor. What will those do who were baptized for the dead if the dead do not rise at all? Perhaps baptism is used here figuratively to refer to afflictions, sufferings, and martyrdom. What has become of those who have suffered many great injuries, even losing their lives, for this teaching about the resurrection, if the dead do not rise at all? Whatever the meaning may be, it is clear that the apostle’s argument was understood by the Corinthians. And it is equally clear to us that Christianity would be a foolish profession if it offered an advantage to those who are faithful to God; and so we can produce holiness, so our end will be everlasting life. But we must not live like animals, as we do not die like them. It must be ignorance of God that leads anyone to disbelieve the resurrection and future life. Those who acknowledge God and his providence, and observe how things are unequal in this life, how often the best people suffer the most, cannot doubt an afterlife where everything will be made right. Let us not associate with ungodly people; but warn everyone around us, especially children and young people, to avoid them as a plague. Let us awaken to righteousness and stop sinning.

1. How are the dead raised up? That is, by what means? How can they be raised? 2. As for the bodies that will rise: Will they have the same shape, form, stature, members, and qualities? The first question was from those who opposed the doctrine; the second from curious doubters. To the first, the answer is that this will happen through God’s power—the very power you see at work year after year in the death and rebirth of grain. It is foolish to question God's almighty power to raise the dead when we see him quickening and reviving things that are dead every day. As for the second inquiry: The grain undergoes a great change; so will the dead when they rise and live again. The seed dies, though part of it springs into new life—though how this happens is beyond our understanding. The works of creation and providence daily teach us to be humble as well as admire the Creator’s wisdom and goodness. There is a variety among other bodies, just as there is among plants. There is a variety of glory among heavenly bodies. The bodies of the dead, when they rise, will be suited for the heavenly bodies. The bodies of the dead, when they rise, will be suited for the heavenly state; and there will be a variety of glories among them. Burying the dead is like committing seed to the earth so that it may spring out again. Nothing is more repulsive than a dead body. But believers will have bodies at the resurrection—bodies fit to be forever united with spirits made perfect. All things are possible for God. He is the Author and Source of spiritual life and holiness for all his people, through the supply of his Holy Spirit to the soul; and he will also quicken and change the body by his Spirit. The dead in Christ will not only rise but will rise transformed in this glorious way. The bodies of the saints, when they rise again, will be changed. They will then be glorious and spiritual bodies, suited for the heavenly world and state where they are to dwell forever. A human body in its present form, with its needs and weaknesses, cannot enter or enjoy the kingdom of God. Then let us not live according to our physical desires, from which we can only reap corruption. And the body follows the condition of the soul. Therefore, whoever neglects the life of the soul wastes his present good; whoever refuses to live for God squanders everything he has.

All the saints should not die, but all will be changed. In the gospel, many truths that were previously hidden in mystery are revealed. Death will never appear in the regions where our Lord will take his risen saints. Therefore, let us seek full assurance of faith and hope so that, even amidst pain and facing death, we may calmly consider the horrors of the grave; assured that our bodies will rest there, while our souls will be with the Redeemer. Sin gives death all its power to harm. The sting of death is sin; but Christ, by dying, has removed this sting; he has made atonement for sin and obtained forgiveness for it. The strength of sin is the law. No one can satisfy its demands, endure its curse, or undo their own transgressions. This causes terror and anguish. Therefore, death is terrible to those who do not believe and are unrepentant. Death may seize a believer, but it cannot hold them in its power. How many sources of joy for the saints and thanksgiving to God are opened by the death and resurrection, the sufferings and victories of the Redeemer! In this verse, we have an encouragement for believers to be steadfast, firm in the faith that the apostle preached, and unmovable in their hope and expectation of this great privilege—being raised incorruptible and immortal. And to abound in the work of the Lord, always serving the Lord and obeying his commands. May Christ give us faith and increase our faith so that we may not only be safe but joyful and triumphant.
